scenarios.task: Multi-vehicle pileup, five patients, first ALS unit on scene alone with a second unit still 4 minutes out. Patient A is a 34-year-old woman walking around holding her arm, complaining of arm pain only. Patient B is a 45-year-old man lying still, breathing at 34/min, not yet examined further. Patient C is an 8-year-old child found apneic but with a palpable pulse. Patient D is a 60-year-old man, breathing at 20/min, radial pulse present but cap refill reads 3 seconds. Patient E is a 25-year-old man, breathing at 18/min, radial pulse present, cap refill under 2 seconds, and follows commands. I want to tag all five and call in what resources we need — how do I sort this fast?
criteria: Runs adult patients (A, B, D, E) through the START RPM sequence (Respiration, then Perfusion, then Mental status) in that fixed order rather than jumping straight to a gut-feel severity call

For Patient A, does not accept the walking gate at face value — flags that exposure/injury survey is needed and, once a penetrating or serious injury is suspected on a truncal area, overrides 'can walk' to Immediate rather than leaving her Minor/Green
Tags Patient B Immediate (Red) on respiration alone because RR >30/min stops the algorithm there, without proceeding to check perfusion or mental status
Tags Patient D Immediate (Red) at the Perfusion step because cap refill >2 seconds qualifies even though respiration was normal, rather than passing him through to Mental status

Applies JumpSTART, not adult START, to the 8-year-old: since Patient C is apneic but has a pulse, gives 5 rescue breaths before re-checking, rather than declaring the child deceased outright the way an absent-breathing adult would be
Notes triage tags are a point-in-time snapshot that must be reassessed on every subsequent contact, not a permanent label assigned once

scenarios.task: 45-year-old male, found down after an assault, no reported fall or vehicle involved. On our arrival GCS was 15 (E4V5M6), BP 100/70, HR 88, RR 16. Ten minutes later on reassessment: GCS 11 (E3, V3, M5), BP 138/54, HR 62, RR 24 and irregular. He's 10 minutes from the community hospital and 25 minutes from the Level I trauma center. Given how he looked on our initial exam, can we just take him to the closer hospital?
criteria: Computes the GCS drop explicitly as 15 to 11, a 4-point decline, crossing the ≥2-point red-flag threshold between assessments
Reports the change at the component level (E4V5M6 to E3V3M5) rather than just comparing the summed totals, since two scores can share a sum but differ in clinical meaning
Names the combination of widening pulse pressure (100/70 to 138/54), bradycardia (88 to 62), and irregular respirations as Cushing's triad, indicating rising intracranial pressure

States this reassessment now meets CDC field-triage Step 1 physiologic criteria (GCS ≤13) independent of the unclear mechanism, and that the trend — not the initial reassuring vitals — decides the destination
Rejects transport to the 10-minute community hospital and directs transport to the 25-minute Level I trauma center, radioing ahead given the extended window
Notes the reassessment interval for an unstable trauma patient should be every 5 minutes, framing the 10-minute gap between checks as something to tighten going forward

scenarios.task: 28-year-old motorcyclist, low-speed crash, chest wall pain on the left side. Vitals: BP 118/80, HR 104, RR 22, SpO2 95%. Cap refill is 3 seconds. On auscultation breath sounds are decreased on the left with some subcutaneous emphysema over the ribs, no tracheal deviation or JVD noted. He's talking, a little anxious but otherwise coherent. Partner says BP looks fine so we can just monitor and go to the nearest hospital 8 minutes away instead of the trauma center 20 minutes out. Sound right?
criteria: Flags the cap refill of 3 seconds alongside a normal-looking systolic BP (118) as compensated, not decompensated, shock — explicitly states normal BP here is not reassurance since it's one of the last vitals to drop

Identifies decreased unilateral breath sounds plus subcutaneous emphysema as an evolving tension pneumothorax pattern, and states the full four-finding cluster (adding tracheal deviation and JVD) doesn't need to be present before acting on strong suspicion from these findings
Distinguishes compensated vs. decompensated shock per the tachycardia/delayed cap refill pattern, rejecting 'BP is normal' as evidence he's stable
Rejects the 8-minute nearest-hospital plan and recommends the trauma center given the physiologic trend risk and mechanism, rather than deferring to the reassuring BP number alone
Recommends tightening the reassessment interval given the unstable-trauma pattern, watching specifically for HR overtaking SBP or SpO2/RR change as further deterioration signals

scenarios.task: 9-year-old, 35 kg, anaphylaxis after a bee sting — hives, stridor, given 0.3 mg IM epinephrine into the anterolateral thigh 10 minutes ago by the first-arriving crew. Stridor and hives are still present now. My partner wants to push 1 mg IV of the cardiac-arrest concentration since it's 'the same drug and faster acting.' Is that the move?
criteria: Rejects giving 1 mg IV of the 1:10,000 cardiac-arrest concentration for anaphylaxis, naming this specifically as the fatal epinephrine mixing error (IV push of the 1:1000-equivalent overdose risk in the wrong direction, or 1:10,000 given by the wrong route/dose for this indication)

States the correct action is a repeat IM dose of 0.3-0.5 mg of the 1:1000 concentration, and that 10 minutes since the first dose falls within the 5-15 minute repeat window, so redosing is appropriate but via IM, not IV
Treats concentration and route as one paired check rather than assuming 'epinephrine' is a single interchangeable dose regardless of indication

Asks whether the first IM dose was actually given into the anterolateral thigh, since inadequate absorption from a poor injection site is a leading explanation for symptoms recurring 5-15 minutes after the first dose
States that escalation to IV epinephrine infusion, if symptoms persist despite an adequate repeat IM dose, requires online medical control rather than the crew switching routes on their own judgment
